title: Statistical tests for non-independent partitions of large autocorrelated datasets
author: Ives, Zhu, et al.

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: A single dataset, or a subset of a larger dataset, in which errors are autocorrelated can be analyzed by GLS when V is known [10]. Thus, let Y denote an N x 1 vector of response variables, and X denote an N x p matrix of predictor variables including a column of ones for the intercept. The p x 1 vector B^ containing the estimates of the regression coefficients B is(2)B^=(X′V−1X)−1(X′V−1Y)

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: An F-test is based on the sum-of-squared error of the full model (SSE) and a reduced model (SSE0) defined by the hypothesis to be tested; for example, the null hypothesis that some of the predictor variables have no effect on the response variable is tested using the reduced model with these predictor variables removed. Under the null hypothesis, and letting SSR1–0 = SSR1 – SSR0 = SSE0 – SSE denote the difference in sum-of-squared regression between full and reduced models, we have(4)(SSR1−0/df1)/(SSE/df2)∼F

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: Thus, the ratio of SSR1–0/df1 to SSE/df2 follows an F distribution where df1 is the degrees of freedom equaling the difference in the number of predictor variables between full and reduced models, and df2 = N – p is the degrees of freedom for the sum-of-squared error in the full model. Strategic selection of the reduced model makes it possible to test a wide range of hypotheses, not only about whether one or more of the coefficients in B are different from zero, but also whether some or all of the coefficients are equal by generating orthogonal contrasts [12].

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: For GLS, a LRT is closely related to an F-test. Specifically, the log-likelihood ratio between the full and reduced models is SSR1–0 for the case when the full and reduced models have the same covariance matrix V. The LRT uses the asymptotic approximation(5)2SSR1−0∼χdf1

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: Thus, twice the difference between the SSRs from the full and reduced models is χ2 distributed with df1 degrees of freedom. The distribution for the F-test (Eq. 4) will converge to the distribution for the LRT when df2 approaches infinity. For large datasets, df2 will be large, and the F-test and LRT will give very similar results.

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: Like the LRT, the t-test is closely related to the F-test. Letting MSE = SSE/df2 be the mean squared error, the estimated covariance matrix for the estimates B^ is(6)var^[B^]=MSE(X′V−1X)−1

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: The standard error of the estimate of a coefficient bh contained in B^, se[b^h], is the square-root of the hth diagonal element of var^ [B^], and a test of the null hypothesis that the coefficient is zero is performed with t-distribution:(7)b^h/se[b^h]∼tdf2

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: To simplify the following developments, it is useful to recast the GLS model above by transforming variables. Specifically, let D be the matrix such that DVD′ = I. For example, D could be the inverse of the Cholesky decomposition of V. With this construction of D, the covariance matrix for the transformed errors A = DΓ is E[AA′] = E[(DΓ)(DΓ)′] = DVD′ = I. If U = DX and Z = DY, then(8)B^=(U'U)−1(U'Z)

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: Further, let H denote the hat matrix for U defined by(9)H=U(U′U)−1U′

steps.title: GLS analysis
steps.instruction: Then(10)SSR1−0=Z′(H−H0)Z(11)SSE=Z′(I−H)Zwhere H0 is the hat matrix for the reduced model derived from U0 = DX0, and X0 is the matrix of predictor variables in the reduced model.
